explanations.1: The endocardium is the smooth inner lining of the heart chambers and does not directly prevent backflow. Chordae tendineae, papillary muscles, and AV valves all work together to prevent blood from flowing backward into the atria.
explanations.2: The mitral valve, also known as the bicuspid valve, is located between the left atrium and the left ventricle. The tricuspid valve is on the right side, and the pulmonary and aortic valves are semilunar valves leading out of the ventricles.
explanations.3: Blood flows from the vena cava into the right atrium, through the tricuspid valve, to the right ventricle, then through the pulmonary semilunar valve. After oxygenation in the lungs, blood returns to the left atrium, passes through the bicuspid (mitral) valve, into the left ventricle, and finally through the aortic semilunar valve.
explanations.4: The right atrium is the first chamber to receive deoxygenated blood returning from the systemic circuit via the superior and inferior venae cavae. The left atrium receives oxygenated blood from the pulmonary circuit.
explanations.5: The endocardium, specifically its endothelial cells, secretes various chemicals including powerful vasoconstrictors like endothelin, which influence the local ionic environment and strength of cardiac contraction. The other layers (pericardial sac, myocardium, epicardium) have different primary functions.
explanations.6: The left ventricle has the thickest myocardium because it is responsible for pumping blood at high pressure into the aorta and throughout the entire systemic circulation. The other chambers pump against lower resistance or over shorter distances.
explanations.7: While typically functionally closed, a small remnant of the foramen ovale (patent foramen ovale) can persist in the interatrial septum in a significant percentage of healthy adults. The other septa are normally fully closed in adults.
explanations.8: Cardiac muscle cells possess autorhythmicity, meaning they can spontaneously generate their own action potentials without external nervous stimulation, a unique feature for maintaining rhythmic contraction. Sarcoplasmic reticulum and gap junctions are also found in other muscle types, and high mitochondria concentration is not exclusive to cardiac muscle.
explanations.9: The plateau phase of the cardiac muscle cell action potential is primarily caused by the sustained influx of calcium ions (Ca2+) through slow voltage-gated channels. Sodium influx causes depolarization, and potassium efflux causes repolarization.
explanations.10: Atrial repolarization occurs simultaneously with ventricular depolarization (QRS complex) and its electrical activity is masked by the larger electrical signal generated by the ventricles. Therefore, it does not produce a distinct wave on the ECG.
explanations.11: The Purkinje fibers have the slowest intrinsic rate of depolarization (20-40 bpm) among the components of the heart's conduction system, serving as the last potential pacemaker if higher centers fail. The SA node is the fastest, followed by the AV node.
explanations.12: Isovolumic contraction is the phase during ventricular systole when the ventricles are contracting, but all heart valves are closed, so no blood is ejected. Systole is general contraction, diastole is general relaxation, and quiescent is a resting period.
explanations.13: Most ventricular filling (approximately 70-80 percent) occurs passively during early and mid-ventricular diastole, when the atria are also in diastole and the AV valves are open. Atrial systole contributes only the final 20-30 percent.
explanations.14: The first heart sound (S1 or 'lub') is caused by the closing of the atrioventricular (AV) valves (tricuspid and mitral) at the beginning of ventricular systole. The closing of semilunar valves causes the second heart sound.
explanations.15: Ventricular relaxation (diastole) immediately follows ventricular repolarization (represented by the T wave on an ECG), as the electrical relaxation phase triggers the mechanical relaxation of the ventricular muscle cells.
explanations.16: Afterload is the resistance or pressure that the ventricles must overcome to eject blood into the major arteries (aorta and pulmonary trunk). Preload is the degree of stretch on the ventricles, while cardiac output and stroke volume are measures of blood pumped.
explanations.17: The primary cardiovascular centers, which regulate heart rate and contractility, are located in the medulla oblongata of the brainstem. The pons, midbrain, and cerebrum have different primary functions in cardiovascular regulation.
explanations.18: In a healthy young adult, when heart rate increases significantly above 160 bpm, cardiac output begins to decrease because the excessively shortened ventricular filling time leads to a substantial reduction in stroke volume, despite the faster rate.
explanations.19: Venous constriction decreases the capacity of the veins, which are blood reservoirs, thereby increasing venous return to the heart. This increased return leads to a greater end-diastolic volume, which is defined as preload.
explanations.20: Calcium ions (Ca2+) are positive inotropes because an increase in their intracellular concentration directly enhances the force of myocardial contraction. High potassium (K+) can decrease contractility, and sodium (Na+) is primarily involved in depolarization.
explanations.21: The heart is the first functional organ to develop in the human embryo, beginning to pump blood approximately three weeks into gestation. Other organs like the brain, stomach, and lungs develop and become functional later.
explanations.22: The heart develops from the mesoderm, which is one of the three primary germ layers in the early embryo, specifically from cells within the cardiogenic region of the mesoderm. Ectoderm and endoderm give rise to different tissues and organs.
explanations.23: During early embryonic development, two endocardial tubes (or cardiogenic cords) form in the cardiogenic region and then fuse to create the single primitive heart tube. The cardiogenic region is the area, not the tubes themselves.
explanations.24: The bulbus cordis, a region of the primitive heart tube, is responsible for giving rise to the right ventricle of the mature heart. The primitive ventricle gives rise to the left ventricle.
explanations.25: The truncus arteriosus is a primitive heart structure that undergoes division during development to form the ascending aorta and the pulmonary trunk, the two great arteries that leave the heart.
